question: A team is migrating off the deprecated `common.keycloak.*` namespace. They previously set `common.keycloak.url` and `common.keycloak.realm`. What does the Camunda 8.8 reference prescribe?

options.text: Set `camunda.client.auth.token-url` to the issuer URL and drop `realm` — there is no Keycloak-specific configuration for Camunda; the issuer is provided as a URL.
Set `camunda.client.keycloak.url` and `camunda.client.keycloak.realm` — Keycloak remains a first-class identity provider with its own namespace.
Rename `common.keycloak.realm` to `camunda.client.auth.realm` — only `realm` is renamed; `url` continues to work unchanged.
Keep `common.keycloak.url` and `common.keycloak.realm` — they are still the recommended way to point at Keycloak in self-managed deployments.

a.text: The deprecation table maps both `common.keycloak.url` and `common.keycloak.token-url` to `camunda.client.auth.token-url`, with the explicit hint that there is no Keycloak-specific configuration for Camunda — the issuer is provided as a URL. `common.keycloak.realm` has no replacement entry for the same reason.

b.text: There is no `camunda.client.keycloak.*` namespace in the reference; the docs state plainly that Keycloak-specific configuration is no longer used by Camunda.

c.text: `realm` is not renamed under `auth.realm`. The docs collapse Keycloak's `<base>/realms/<realm>/...` structure into the single issuer URL the user supplies.

d.text: Both properties are listed in the deprecation table, so they are not the recommended ongoing way to configure auth.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 treats the OAuth issuer as an opaque URL set via `camunda.client.auth.token-url`. The legacy `common.keycloak.*` properties — including `realm`, which has no direct replacement — were specific to one identity provider and are deprecated; the issuer URL is now the only Keycloak-related setting needed.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter - properties reference
